item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
That's fine, though I would add that I don't think the point was for them to be funny in the first place.

Perhaps he meant that it's no longer as successful. Dunno.

682 is really not a very good article, but judging only by the merits, it's one of the best. That's why I think "judging by merit" is fair, because certain articles will come on top not because of their quality, but because of circumstance.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I think morhadow here is trying to say that "milk articles are no longer so funny."

That's fine, though I would add that I don't think the point was for them to be funny in the first place.

Regarding 682: I don't think it's a very good article. It was written during a time of much more lax standards. Various reimaginations of 682 have achieved what it failed to achieve since then (see: Summer's Exile, The Lucky Dinosaur, Impossible-To-Destroy Reptile).

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I think morhadow here is trying to say that "milk articles are no longer so funny." Perhaps a different way to say it is "This specific archetype of milk-related SCPs seems dry."

Also:

This article's content should be scrutinized on its own merits, not in comparison to something else.

So, SCP-682 is an excellent article, and regarding merits, much better than the entirety of the Ouroboros Proposal. I don't think that's the right way to go about it.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
This is the proof that the milk meme has been dried up.

To be fair, I don't think Laneous or Clem or I ever decided to write milk-related articles in service of a meme. I don't know where that observation came from but it's horribly simplistic, and I'm not quite sure what it means exactly from a critical standpoint. If your assertion is that this article is for a meme, it ignores the sheer amount of creativity put into this particular article apart from milk.

I can't deny that SCP-4487 should have gotten a lot more crit and feedback — for both its narrative structure and core concept — than what it received, and ought to have some further polishing based on the criticisms mentioned this discussion. But I think what was written here is really, genuinely imaginative, even if it isn't executed as well as it could have been.

At the same time, I'll point out that criticizing this article for supposedly not achieving what another article did achieve is missing the point. This article's content should be scrutinized on its own merits, not in comparison to something else.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
This is the proof that the milk meme has been dried up.

Preach it, brother.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: What a shame, I was planning on making an article about "A land flowing with milk and honey."

This is the proof that the milk meme has been dried up.

First we had SCP-3739, an excellent article tying pataphysics, broken masquerade, milk and horror.

Second we had SCP-4475. While I like seeing fifthism and ortothan mythos, this felt like 3739 2: Honey Edition IN SPACE. Also, the sister article is better for me.

And we have SCP-4487, which is the combination of a superficial reading of 3739 and trying to rewrite a coldpost. It's true that now this is nearer to a proper SCP than the prior coldpost, but the format screw the Ecto department, the predictable and a bit of cartoonish made me downvote.

Nice CSS theme

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the format screw, but found the encounter log to drag on, and found myself losing interest midway through it. I really want to upvote this but feel like it needs to be trimmed down or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't believe that there's an image like that out there of a gown-wearing cow skull on Wikimedia.

item.title: Re: No Vote
item.content:encoded: Oh boy do I want to end this better, I'm actually thinking about removing the fourth Addendum entirely as it may be part of ruining the ending, due to it revealing way too much. At the same time, I don't have a good idea onto how to change the plot's ending to anything different then it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, however, does work against the overall quality. This is what's known in the industry as 'author preemption.'

I knew this would be considered author preemption in some way, I just wanted to give this dimension some character of it's own to make it stand out from the normal Foundation. I felt as if just slapping a wacky class on it and calling it good would make this seem less like a alt-dimension overall.

but also gives off the impression that we are expected to forgive mistake after mistake (some of which include basic grammar and spelling) because this is an alternate universe and because the author said to not mind these.

Those grammatical errors are actually unintentional, and I must have missed them whilst going through this. I'll start trying to eat them up.

item.title: No Vote
item.content:encoded:
Allow me to explain my reasoning for no-voting this article.

I, personally, did not have as much of an issue with the tone and disclaimer in comparison to Veiedhimaedhr (above). I saw it as charming, in a sense. However, despite this, there are a number of grammatical mistakes, and sentence structure could be better.

My main issues here, though, are the with plot and the ending.

The beginning starts with an interesting premise, a custom CSS theme, and a creepy image. Good start. However, as the article continues, the plot begins to boil down from "strange, mysterious, world-ending anomaly that jumps dimensions" to "evil milk people steal milk and flood realities." That wouldn't be so bad in of itself, but the way it was executed made the article sub-par. At the end, we expect what happens, since the only logical conclusion is that "oh no now we're going to die too." That is, in my opinion, the pitfall of the article, and a cliche that is too commonly used.

However, as much as I criticized the article, I did nonetheless enjoy it to an extent. That is why I am not downvoting it. It seems as though this piece will survive on the wiki, but I would personally recommend revamping the plot in order to tell a better story overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So we've got an alternate dimension Foundation's article, and a creepy looking image to greet us right off the bat. Interesting premise.

+ show block
– hide block

Object Class: Spiritual

Whelp. But ultimately this is just a classification and doesn't do much from a literary standpoint so, honestly, it counts neither towards or against the overall quality, despite what some may say.

SCPF-LZPP87 is noticeably more loosely-organized and small-scale. Documentation as a result is rough and prone to inconsistencies in formatting and tone.

This, however, does work against the overall quality. This is what's known in the industry as 'author preemption.' Pretty much, the author steps into the work and talks directly to the reader asking them to forgive something that they're about to see. In this case, the author steps in and tells us that clinical tone, formatting and so on is inconsistent but lol don't worry its because alternate universe. Generally speaking, it's not a good sign to see inconsistent writing acknowledged by the author before we even begin reading the primary story.

Rosie: Stay out of this heretics, this is far beyond your udderstanding [sic].

Grammatically incorrect. There should be a comma after 'this' since heretics are the receivers of that command. Beyond that, it's really difficult to take any antagonistic force seriously when they start spinning out puns. But like the object class, characterization is characterization. My dislike of puns is minor in comparison to:

(Through teeth.) Shut up.

I know that we should be ignoring 'inconsistent' tone thanks to the disclaimer but imagine the person who watched the magicamera footage tasked to record this important incident hearing this and going 'I'm gonna describe this creatively.'

Daisy's abdominal mouth snaps at the book in M9-2's hand. As he attempts to pludge his sword into the mouth, a large spike of calcified rock extends from the mouth, destabilizing the sword and causing it to demanifest. M9-2 grabs Daisy by the mouth's teeth and throws her against the wall, damaging it. He kicks Daisy in the gut several more times, and the wall cracks further.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Plunge is spelled incorrectly. The sarcastic banter from the task force members, the pun/mustache-twirling pun cultists and the purely informal, action-adventure narration prose combines into a chunk of reading that is not just wholly uninteresting, but also gives off the impression that we are expected to forgive mistake after mistake (some of which include basic grammar and spelling) because this is an alternate universe and because the author said to not mind these.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so delightfully bizarre, I love it, and the fact that you were able to turn "Spiritual" from a coldpost meme into a legitimate object classification is both hilarious and admirable. Good experimental work here.

Long live The Great Udder! Long live Milk!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
THE CLASS IS BACK BABY

This is not a rewrite of LaserPewPew's infamous spiritual horse, I just wanted to see if I could make this class work. Let's see if I did.

Thanks to FloppyPhoenix for making sure this is up to Milk SpecificationsTM.

Theme code by ashburystreet, edited a little by me to make some stuff look different.

Images and Research:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Milk_Lake.jpg

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cegua.JPG

https://www.ag.ndsu.edu/stutsmancountyextension/news-articles/dairy-terminology

Also i'm aware that it's cringe that the theme is localized on the page, I literally spent two nights trying to get include and/or import working.
